coredata.dc:title: Alginate‑carbon dot nanocomposite: A green approach towards designing turn-on aptasenor for Candida albicans fungus
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Rahmatian, Nasrin
Abbasi, Shahryar
Abbasi, Naser
Tavakkoli Yaraki, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Candida albicans fungus, an opportunistic microorganism that becomes a pathogen in its host, can often cause superficial and severe infections in human body. In this work, a turn-on fluorescent aptasenor based on Alginate‑carbon dot nanocomposite has been designed through a green approach. Carbon dots (CDs) with average size of 4 ± 1 nm were synthesized hydrothermally using Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract as precursor. Then, mixture of carbon dot and alginate was cross-linked by Ca2+ ions to make Alginate-CD nanocomposite with negligible effect on fluorescence intensity of CDs (λex = 360 nm, λem = 400 nm). The fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime were found as 4.1 % and 3.15 ns, respectively. The nanocomposite was characterize using different spectroscopic (Photoluminescence, UV–visible, XPS, FT-IR) and microscopic (TEM and SEM) analytical techniques. An aptamer was used to selectively target (1 → 3)-β-D-glucan on the surface of the Candida albicans cell wall. It was found that the fluorescence energy resonance transfer between nanocomposite and aptamer quenches the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite, making it suitable for designing turn-on fluorescent probe. It was found that the fluorescence could be recovered in the presence of Candida albicans fungus (in <30 min), owing to higher affinity between aptamer and (1 → 3)-β-D-glucan on the surface of Candida albicans fungus, where there were two linear ranges: low concentration of 50–200 cells/mL and high concentration of 1000–6000 cells/mL (R2 ≥ 0.989 for both linear ranges), with limit of detection of 40 cells/mL. The selectivity of the designed probe was examined against a mixture of microbial agents (Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli) and Curdlan polysaccharide (1 → 3)-b-D-Glucan (L). The designed biosensor showed an excellent ability to directly detect Candida albicans fungus via a simple and fast (<30 min) approach. The results of this experimental study show that Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite is a bright prospect in clinical applications and a suitable alternative to traditional methods of Candida albicans diagnosis.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Green synthesis
Aptamer
Candida albicans
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of aptamer to alginate-CD nanocomposite
Detection and quantification of Candida albicans fungus

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic illustration of the green synthesis of CDs using Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract, b) The absorbance spectrum of the as-synthesized CDs, c) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of the as-synthesized CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
a) TEM image of CDs, b) Hydrodynamic size distribution of CDs, c) Full X-ray photoelectron spectrum of CDs, d) X-ray photoelectron spectrum of C(1s), e) O(1s), and f) N(1s).

a) FTIR spectra for Alginate hydrogel, CD and Alginate-CD nanocomposite, b) XRD patterns of CD and Alginate-CD nanocomposite, c) Schematic illustration of Alginate-CD nanocomposite: preparation and structure, d) The absorbance spectra of Alginate hydrogel and Alginate-CD nanocomposite, and e) The fluorescence spectra for CD and Alginate-CD nanocomposite (λex = 360 nm).
XPS analysis of Alginate-CD nanocomposite: a) full spectrum, b) C(1s), c) O(1s), d) N(1s), and e) Ca(2p).

a) Effect of aptamer concentration on fluorescence intensity of Alginate-CD nanocomposite. 1950 μL of Alginate-CD nanocomposite and PBS buffer (535 μL, pH = 7.4) with different aptamer concentrations (0,10, 12,14,16,18,20,24,28,30 nM, 15 μL) was used in this experiment. The final volume of the solution was kept constant at 2500 μL. b) corresponding relative fluorescence intensity as function of the concentration of aptamer. The incubation time was fixed at 30 min. c) Effect of incubation time on fluorescence intensity of Alginate-CD nanocomposite. d) The corresponding relative fluorescence intensity as a function of incubation time. e) Stern-Volmer plot for the obtained fluorescence data. f) The relative fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite before and after incubation with aptamer at optimized conditions. The standard deviation for each data point is for n-3 replicates.

a) Schematic of identification of Candida albicans fungus by Alginate-CD-aptamer a mechanism on-off-on nanocomposite. b) FRET mechanism in CD fluorescent Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite based on fluorescent CD and electrostatic interaction between aptamer and the nanocomposite.

a) The effect of incubation time of Candida albicans with Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite on recovery of fluorescence spectra. b) Recovered fluorescence intensity ratio as a function of incubation time. The concentration of Candida albicans was 2000 cells/mL. 1965 μL of Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite and PBS buffer (525 μL, pH = 7.4). The final volume of the solution was kept constant at 2500 μL for part a and b. c) The recovered fluorescence intensity ratio as a function of the concentration of Candida albicans (12–25–35-40-50-60-75-95-120-200-250-500-1000-2000-4000-6000 cells/mL). The incubation time was 26 min and also the final volume of the solution remained constant at 2500 μL. The standard deviation was calculated based on three replicates. d) Selectivity study of Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite in the presence of Curdlan polysaccharide (1 → 3)-b-D-Glucan (L) (5 mM, 10 μL), Aspergillus niger fungus (10 μL, 2000 cells/mL), freshly cultivated Staphylococcus aureus (10 μL, 0.5 McFarland) and Escherichia coli bacteria (10 μL, 0.5 McFarland), the fungus Candida albicans (2000 cells/mL, final concentration) and the fungus Candida albicans. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were λex = 360 nm and λem = 400 nm respectively. The incubation time was 26 min and the final volume of the solution remained constant at 2500 mL.
Summary of biosensors with different limits of detection (LOD) for the detection of Candida albicans.

row.entry: Candida. albicans, C. krusei, Candida. tropicalis, and Candida. parapsilosis
Lectin (ConA/ WGA) Concanavalin A (ConA) and wheat germ agglutinin (WGA)
AurisID® kit (with DNA exclusive Candida auris)

head.ce:title: Alginate‑carbon dot nanocomposite: A green approach towards designing turn-on aptasenor for Candida albicans fungus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Natural Sciences, Faculty of Science and Engineering, Macquarie University, NSW 2109, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Natural Sciences, Faculty of Science and Engineering, Macquarie University, NSW 2109, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Candida albicans fungus, an opportunistic microorganism that becomes a pathogen in its host, can often cause superficial and severe infections in human body. In this work, a turn-on fluorescent aptasenor based on Alginate‑carbon dot nanocomposite has been designed through a green approach. Carbon dots (CDs) with average size of 4 ± 1 nm were synthesized hydrothermally using Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract as precursor. Then, mixture of carbon dot and alginate was cross-linked by Ca2+ ions to make Alginate-CD nanocomposite with negligible effect on fluorescence intensity of CDs (λex = 360 nm, λem = 400 nm). The fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime were found as 4.1 % and 3.15 ns, respectively. The nanocomposite was characterize using different spectroscopic (Photoluminescence, UV–visible, XPS, FT-IR) and microscopic (TEM and SEM) analytical techniques. An aptamer was used to selectively target (1 → 3)-β-D-glucan on the surface of the Candida albicans cell wall. It was found that the fluorescence energy resonance transfer between nanocomposite and aptamer quenches the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite, making it suitable for designing turn-on fluorescent probe. It was found that the fluorescence could be recovered in the presence of Candida albicans fungus (in <30 min), owing to higher affinity between aptamer and (1 → 3)-β-D-glucan on the surface of Candida albicans fungus, where there were two linear ranges: low concentration of 50–200 cells/mL and high concentration of 1000–6000 cells/mL (R2 ≥ 0.989 for both linear ranges), with limit of detection of 40 cells/mL. The selectivity of the designed probe was examined against a mixture of microbial agents (Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli) and Curdlan polysaccharide (1 → 3)-b-D-Glucan (L). The designed biosensor showed an excellent ability to directly detect Candida albicans fungus via a simple and fast (<30 min) approach. The results of this experimental study show that Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite is a bright prospect in clinical applications and a suitable alternative to traditional methods of Candida albicans diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Candidiasis is the most common fungal infection in humans and the fourth reported hospital infection [1,2] >17 species of Candida are responsible for creating systemic fungi, acting as opportunistic pathogen, that threaten health, especially in people with underlying factors such as weak immune system, diabetes, etc. [3]. This fungi could cause thrush, vaginitis, skin infection, endocarditis, meningitis, brain abscess and even arthritis in the human host [4,5]. The accurate identification of the infection caused by Candida albicans is of great importance owing to decreasing the side effects of this disease during disease progression ad well as decreasing the chance of antifungal resistance in the patient's body. Therefore, there are different microbiological methods for the diagnosis of Candida albicans, including microscopic identification of the fungus in body fluids and histopathological identification and culture of Candida albicans [6] as well as detection techniques based on antibody/antigen detection. [7] However, these techniques suffer from some limitations such as false negative response in patients with immune system defects and false positive response in non-infected individuals that colonization by Candida in body organs that can cause antibody response. [8–10]. Additionally, these methods suffer from lack of specificity or weakness in sensitivity and are time consuming, and on the other hand, the clinical symptoms of candidiasis are non-specific, which delays the process of candidiasis diagnosis and timely treatment. [11,12]

Carbon nano-data were obtained for the first time in 2004 by chance during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through electrophoresis [13]. This discovery led to extensive studies to exploit the fluorescence properties of carbon dots These nanoparticles have been able to because of their extraordinary properties, including high photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and tunable surface, as well as the availability of abundant and inexpensive precursors. Create significant applications in imaging, drug delivery and theranostic development [14]. In general, one of the things that has a great impact on the final properties of carbon dots is their synthesis method. There are various synthesis methods for producing carbon dots, which generally They can be classified into top-down and bottom-up synthesis methods. In the top-down process, macromolecules are converted into nanoparticles by physical processes, while the bottom-up approach mainly involves polymerization and carbonization. It is the formation of a series of molecules through a chemical reaction [15]. And it is important that the easy synthesis of many available carbon sources, including organic and inorganic materials, has attracted the attention of material science scientists around the world, on the other hand, carbon dot A lot of research has been done on them, with high biocompatibility and higher light stability than flashing and whitening light, low toxicity and their unique properties, they have been able to replace fluorescent materials such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots with disadvantages such as toxicity. and colloidality are used. To find the simplest and most compatible strategy for carbon dot fabrication, including hydrothermal [16] microwave/ultrasonic [17], etc., carbonization technique as the simplest and best way to provide CQD with low cost in is considered Not only the manufacturing route, but also the choice of carbon source is an important factor for its technological applications.

Today, the approach of using green resources as a carbon source and preparing it from methods such as hydrothermal, microwave, etc., is considered for its golden advantages such as being environmentally friendly, simple and one-step with low synthesis cost, which does not release any pollutants into the environment. On the other hand, it is important to investigate new and green precursors for economic synthesis from different sources and different applications such as sonophotocatalytic decomposition. [18] environmental applications for the detection of Zn2+ and Hg2+ elements [19] in the treatment of cancer tumors [20,21] and viral diseases, etc. [22] The data obtained from fluorescent carbon is important. Plants are one of the desirable options in the synthesis of green carbon fluorescent dots due to different carbon compounds such as sugar, organic acids, amino acids, pectin, cellulose, and starch [23].

In general, plant extracts consist of many compounds such as alkaloids, flavonoids, glycosides, etc., which have heteroatoms [24]. The presence of heteroatoms in the precursor of the raw material in the preparation of carbon dots increases the quantum efficiency [25]. The reason for this is the high electronegativity of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, the similarity of the atomic size of nitrogen and carbon to each other, the presence of pairs of free electrons. on nitrogen and its easy transfer to 2sp is the structure of carbon π orbitals [26]. For this purpose, mineral sources such as formamide, ammonia, etc. are added to the reaction medium. However, the use of biomass sources such as plant extract that inherently contain heteroatoms seems to be a good approach owing to their low cost, abundancy, and simple preparation procedures. With this approach, the synthesis of CD from Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract, which is rich in terpenoid compounds, polyphenols and heteroatoms, without any other additive chemicals was successfully carried out in this work. It has not been announced on the preparation of CD from Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract.

To enhance the stability of CDs and prevent fluctuations in the optical properties of CDs in complex colloidal solutions, combination of CDs with natural polymers and making nanohydrogel has been found a great approach. Nanohydrogels are three-dimensional polymeric networks of synthetic and natural materials such as collagen [27], alginate [28,29], chitosan [30], hyaluronic acid [31], elastin [32], heparin [33] etc. These materials have become an important potential in medical and biological fields due to their excellent properties such as stability, biocompatibility, good transparency, toxicity and low cost [34]. Nanocomposite hydrogel with bright buds has shown a new perspective in the field of optical materials to scientific research. [35] The physico-chemical properties of hydrogel such as porosity and flexibility as well as non-covalent bonding interactions, ionic-electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonds, etc. between polymer chains could enable the successful interaction between hydrogel and nanoparticles (i.e., CDs), leading to formation of a three-dimensional hydrogel structure like a safe cage system. In addition, surface functional groups on nanohydrogel could enable successful conjugation of aptamers through electrostatic interaction leading to fluorescence quenching in designing turn-on probes.

In this work, we first report green synthesis and characterization of CDs using Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract as carbon source. Then, the as-synthesized CDs were combined with alginate to form a nanohydrogel, where it was characterized using different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. Finally, the designed fluorescent nanohydrogel was used to design a turn-on fluorescent probe for detection and quantification of Candida albicans by targeting RNA strand in (1,3)-β-D-glucan on surface of Candida albicans. The current study is a new diagnostic tool for pathogenic fungi that was able to identify Candida albicans in the presence of other microorganisms with high selectivity. The results of this work could open new avenues to material scientists and chemists as well as researchers in diagnostic discipline, where designing robust optical biosensors is desirable.
List of chemicals, details of synthesis and culturing procedures, detection method, and instrumentation are presented in Supporting Information.

The extract prepared from Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant was selected as the primary source for the green synthesis of carbon dots by hydrothermal bottom-up approach. Gas chromatography (GC) and GC/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) was employed to analyze and indicate the phytochemical compounds of Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract (Fig. S1a,b), where sesquiterpenes were found as dominant compounds. Table S1 summarizes the chemical components, followed by the shelf-life index, are calculated and their contents based on GC/MS results. The results show the existence of terpenoid compounds, polyphenols, etc. as a carbon source for the green synthesis of CDs.

Fig. 1a illustrates different steps in the green synthesis of CDs using Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant was used as initial source. The as-synthesized CDs were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV- visible absorption spectrum for the synthesized CD had two peaks (Fig. 1b). The appearance of a peak at 213 nm indicates π-π* transitions in sp2 orbitals in the CC bond in the structure of carbon rings. On the other hand, the presence of a peak at 275 nm indicates the n-π* transition could be attributed to the CO bond and functional groups on the surface of CDs [36,37]. The solution of CDs had yellow-brown color, while it emitted green color under UV light (365 nm) illumination. The as-synthesized CDs showed excitation-dependent fluorescence, which is typical property in CDs, owing to different emission traps on the CD surface or quantum effects as well as the effect of size of CD nanoparticles [38] [39]. In particular, it was found that excitation at λex = 360 nm led to maximum fluorescence intensity centered at λem = 400 nm (Fig. 1c) Also fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield was 3.15 ns and 4.1 % (Fig. S1c).

The size and morphology of the CDs were analyzed by transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The TEM image shows spherical nanoparticles with dimensions <5 nm (4 ± 1 nm, Fig. 2a), which is consistent with the results of the hydrodynamic size distribution of CDs obtained by DLS (Fig. 2b). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used as techniques to provide information about the chemical bands presented in the CDs. Analysis of the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows the presence of C, O, and small amounts of N as impurities on the surface, which are three strong peaks at 290.22 eV and 503.16 eV and 394.41 eV, respectively. As can be seen in) Fig. 2d(the surface spectrum of the C(1s) core can be divided into five different bonds including (C-O=C), (CN), (CO), (C=C), and (CC) appeared at 283.59 eV, 284.24 eV, 285.50 eV, 286.48 eV, and 287.66 eV, respectively [40]. These bonds confirm the hybrid structures of sp2 and sp3 and carbon with single and double bonds with oxygen and carbonates and π → π* transition in the CD structure. [41] Based on data presented in Fig. 2e, the spectrum of O(1s) can be divided into five species: (O=C), (OH), (CO), (CO phenolic), and (C-O-C/COOH), which have neen appeared at 531.14 eV,531.74 eV,532.53 eV,533.32 eV,533.70 eV, respectively. These confirm the existence of carboxylic, phenolic and hydroxyl functional groups in the CD structure. Fig. 2f also shows the spectrum of N(1s), which represents three species: (NC), (C=N), and (NH) at 398.55 eV, 399.50 eV, and 400.74 eV, respectively [42], indicating the surface amide groups.

First, we optimized the ratio between sodium alginate and calcium chloride in synthesis of alginate nanoparticles in the absence of any carbon dots. The optimization results indicated that 0.04 % w/v NaAlg and 40 mM CaCl2 (NaAlg:CaCl2 volumetric ratio of 2:1) leads to formation of alginate nanopartciles with small size as well as uniform size distribution, monitored by dynamic light scattrering (Fig. S2).

It was observed that when concentrations of 0.04 % of NaAlg were examined in the presence of different concentrations (40 mM and 20 mM) of calcium chloride, it led to a smaller size of nanoparticles with an increase in the concentration of calcium chloride, which shows this trend with The literature agrees [43]. On the other hand, when the concentration of CaCl2 at 40 mM was chosen to continue the investigation in the presence of different concentrations of NaAlg (0.04 % and0.06 % and 0.08 %).

By reducing the concentration of alginate polyanions, they were smaller in size, which can be attributed to the reduction in the number of carboxylate ions and ether and hydroxyl groups attached to calcium ion (Ca2+) [44,45]. And with the reduction of the number of functional groups around Ca2+, a smaller number of alginate chains appear around this cation, the final result of which is the reduction of the average size of these particles. [46] The results of these adjustments in the (Fig. S2f), which clearly shows the average size of nanoparticles in each of the different concentrations of NaAlg and CaCl2, increases the concentration of Ca2+ ions and decreases the concentration of NaAlg, and the size of prepared nanoparticles improves significantly. This issue can be due to the relative reduction of the number of functional groups involved with the central ion.

Fig. S3a shows the schematic illustration of the synthesis of the Alginate hydrogels by Ca+2 as crosslinker. SEM image of Alginate nanoparticles (Fig. S3b) shows that they are mainly in the form of quasi-spherical particles with a uniform size distribution. The average particle size was estimated to be 200 ± 7 nm, which is in consistent with DLS results.

FT-IR spectrum is considered to identify functional groups in alginate‑carbon dot structure and alginate‑carbon dot nanocomposite as well as to understand the possible interactions between alginate and CDs (Fig. 3a). The broad peaks at 3253 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of NH and OH functional groups on the surface of CDs, respectively. The bands at 2706 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching groups [47]. The band at 1743 cm−1 is corresponding to the CO stretching group on the surface and also the peak at 1620 cm−1 clearly confirm the CC functional group of the stretching aromatic rings in the CD structure. The peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1 are correlated with the bending vibration of CH bonds in aldehydes and alkane, respectively. The peak at 1210 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching group of the ester, and the bands at 1107 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 belong to the C-OH and O-CO stretching groups in CD, respectively [48]. The band at 760 cm−1 is attributed to the CC bend and the peak at 605 cm−1 is related to the halide group, which could be found in the plant extract. The presence of NH and OH groups on surface of CDs confirm the hydrophilicity of CDs, and their dispersibility in water.

The FTIR spectrum of Alginate hydrogel possesses a broad peak at 3448 cm−1, which represents the OH stretching functional group. The weak bands at 2922 cm−1 and 2846 cm−1 are related to CH stretching vibrations, and the peak at 1629 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching group of CC rings. These bands confirm the aromaticity in Alginate hydrogel structure. The band at 1421 cm−1 is due to C-OH deformation vibration with the help of O-C-O symmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate group in Alginate hydrogel [49]. The absorption band at 1020 cm−1 is CO stretching group in the pyranose ring and other ether groups support the structure of Alginate hydrogel [50]. The strong and broad peaks at 3265 cm−1and 3444 cm−1 in Alginate-CD nanocomposite are related to the strong stretching vibrations of OH in Alginate hydrogel and CD structure, as well as the NH functional group in CDs. The bands at 2954 cm−1 and 2826 cm−1 are related to CH stretching groups in alkane and aldehyde in the Alginate-CD nanocomposite structure, and the band at 1731 cm−1 is the CO stretching group on the surface of Alginate-CD nanocomposite. The sharp peak at 1626 cm−1 is the CC alkene stretching group in aromatic compounds in the nanocomposite. Additionally, the bands at 1263 cm−1and 1030 cm−1 indicate the CO stretching groups of ester and CO stretching group in pyranose ring and ether in the structure of Alginate-CD nanocomposite.

The phase and crystal structure of alginate hydrogel and synthesized alginate-CD nanocomposite were investigated by XRD technique (Fig. 3b). The X-ray pattern for CDs shows two peaks at 2θ =23.4° and 43.2° and one peak at 13.3°, which are related to (002), (101) and (110) planes of graphitic carbon plates, respectively, confirming an amorphous structure [51]. The diffraction pattern for Alginate-CD nanocomposite has a sharp peak at 2θ =13.4° corresponding to (110) plane due to the close arrangement of alginate molecules. Additionally, the peaks at 2θ =21.2° and 30.1° are due to the distance between the layers, and they illustrate the chain direction of alginate molecules [52]. The appearance of the peaks at 2θ = 11.3°, 13.4°, 22.4°, and 45.3° plates clearly confirms the successful existence of CD structure in the Alginate-CD nanocomposite. Based on the obtained results, a schematic illustration of the Alginate-CD nanocomposite, the structure, and chemical bond of each component could be shown as Fig. 3c.

To investigate the optical properties of Alginate-CD nanocomposite, the absorbance and fluorescence spectra of the as-synthesized nanocomposite were measured. Similar to Alginate hydrogel, the UV–visible spectrum Alginate-CD nanocomposite had a broad shoulder from 300 nm to 330 nm to n-π* transitions in CO bonds and a peak at 215 nm to π-π* transitions in CC bonds in the aromatic chemical structure, which indicate the existence of alginate in the structure of nanocomposite (Fig. 3d). On the other hand, in the Alginate-CD nanocomposite, the peak with the intensity and resolution of 282 nm and the other peak at 218 nm are due to this bathochromic interaction between CD and alginate in Alginate-CD nanocomposite. The Alginate-CD nanocomposite showed an emission at λem = 400 nm 400 nm, when it was excited at the λex = 360 nm. As can be seen in (Fig. 3e) the emission intensity was almost comparable with the same amount of CDs with slight improvement without any displacement. This shows that the hydrogel strengthens the radiation hybrid in the surface energy traps, leading to improved fluorescence intensity [53].

To obtain more insight into chemical bonds and surface groups in the Alginate-CD nanocomposite, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed. Full XPS spectrum (Fig. 4a) shows four strong peaks at 289.51 eV, 397.43 eV and 327.54 eV,524.68 eV, which are related to C, N, Ca and O, respectively. The spectrum of C(1 s) can be divided into five types: 283.78 eV (C-O=C), 284.65 eV (CO, CN), 285.81 V eV (C=C), 287.10 eV (CC), and 287.76 eV (CH) (Fig. 4b). These results confirm the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybrids in aromatic and hydrocarbon structures in CD and Alginate hydrogel, as well as carbon bonding with monovalent oxygen and double bonds and π → π* transitions [54].

The spectrum of O(1s) can be divided into five types: 531.3 eV (O=C), 531.74 eV (C-O-C), 532.01 eV (OH), 533.2 eV (CO aromatic), and 534.02 eV (C-O-C, COOH) (Fig. 4c). Oxygen bridging bonds in hydroxyl structure and aromatic structures in Alginate hydrogel also match the presence of carboxyl groups in Alginate hydrogel and CD [55]. The spectrum of N(1s) can be confirmed in three species including 398.55 eV (NC), 399.38 eV (C=N), and 400.88 eV (NH) (Fig. 4d) on the surface of CD in Alginate-CD nanocomposite. The spectrum of Ca(2p) can be divided into two types: 346.62 eV (Ca(2p3/2))and 349.52 eV (Ca(2p1/2)) (Fig. 4e), which indicate the existence of divalent Ca+2 ion in the Alginate hydrogel structure in the nanocomposite structure [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of aptamer to alginate-CD nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
Examining the required incubation time and aptamer concentration to obtain successful conjugation of aptamer with Alginate-CD nanocomposite is necessary step in designing a biosensor. To optimize the amount of aptamer, a fixed amount of Alginate-CD nanocomposite solution (1950 μL) was mixed with different concentrations of aptamer (0 to 30 nM, 15 μL) and PBS Buffer (535 μL, pH = 7.4), followed by incubated for 30 min. The fluorescence emission intensity was recorded for each sample to monitor the adsorption of aptamer on the surface of nanocomposite through non-covalent interactions such as π-π stacking between the ring structures of nucleobases and hexagons of the CD aromatic network, and hydrogen bonds between -OH and -COOH groups on the surface CD and -NH2 and -OH groups in the aptamer (Fig. 5 a,b). The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the donor molecule (CD) to the acceptor molecule (aptamer) results in quenching the fluorescence emission, indicating successful interaction between aptamer and nanocomposite. It was observed that increasing the aptamer concentration beyond 20 nM did not lead to significant change in the fluorescence quenching, therefore this concentratin was considered as optimum concentration for further studies. Under this condition, the fluorescence intensity was monitored at different time intervals, where it was found that 28 min is enough to lead to complete fluorescence quenching (Fig. 5c,d). To further analysis of fluorescence quenching data, which follows the trend of fluorescence quenching with increasing aptamer concentration, we modeled this quenching data with Stern-Volmer equation:
(5)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K SV
φ
where Io and I are the fluorescence intensity in the absence of aptamer and in the presence of aptamer, and φ is the aptamer concentration (i.e., quencher). A plot of Io/I versus
φ
is a line with a slope of K sv. [19] The data show a linear response to the gradual increase in the aptamer concentration, the quenching constant (K sv) was obtained as 0.1612 ×
10 9
M
− 1
(Fig. 5e). Based on the results, the fluorescence emission was quenched by 0.95 % under optimized conditions, which was used for further experiments (Fig. 5f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and quantification of Candida albicans fungus
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent probe (on-off-on) for defection and quantification of Candida albicans fungus can be designed according to the proposed mechanism in Fig. 6 (a,b). Alginate nano hydrogel creates a three-dimensional network by electrostatic and ionic bonding through carboxylic functional groups with calcium ions. When CDs are trapped in the structure of this three-dimensional network, it is protected against environmental effects such aggregation. Therefore, alginate-CD nanocomposite has stable optical properties (λex = 360 nm and λem = 400 nm, ON Mode) and the acceptor (aptamer) leads quenching the fluorescence (OFF Mode). Further, when the CD-aptamer alginate nanocomposite is placed in the presence of the Candida albicans fungus, the aptamer selectively binds to the RNA strand in (1,3)-β-D- Glucan on the surface of Candida albicans through hydrogen bonding, leading to separation of aptamer from surface of nanocomposite and recovery of fluorescence intensity (ON Mode) proportional to the number of fungus. Therefore, this approach could be used for detetion and quantification of Candida albicans fungus.

To detect and quantify Candida albicans fungus, the as-prepared aptamer-conjugated Alginate-CD nanocomposite (1965 μL) and PBS buffer (525 μL, pH = 7.4) was incubated with solution containing Candida albicans fungus (10 μL, 2000 cells/mL) in a complete sterile environment, and the recovered fluorescence spectra was monitored at different time intervals (0–30 min). As can be seen in Fig. 7a,b, the fluorescence recovery became completed after 26 min. Under this condition, the experiment was repeated and the concentration of fungus was tuned in the range of 12–6000 cells/mL. It was found that there are two linear regions for low (50–200 cells/mL) and high (1000–6000 cells/mL) concentrations ranges, while the limit of detection was found as 40 cells/mL (Fig. 7c). To compare the sensitivity of the designed turn-on biosensor with other techniques, Table 1 shows a comparison between sensitivity of the designed turn-on biosensor In this work with other techniques for detection of Candida albicans fungus, indicating that the designed turn-on fluorescent probe in this work could suppress other techniques and bring a new level of sensitivity for detection of Candida albicans fungus.

To investigate the specificity of the designed turn on probe, we tested it against microbial samples and similar to the target analyte. The presence of various types of bacteria and fungi always increases the incidence of bacterial and fungal infections [62]. The difference between fungal and bacterial infections can be examined in different ways. Since bacterial and fungal infections may be mistakenly considered, the wrong prescription of antibiotics in the treatment of fungal infection could lead to destroying some of the bacteria that play a role in inhibiting the growth of yeasts. Additionally, this wrong diagnosis and medication could aggravate the infection in susceptible patients with a weak immune system or diabetes. Therefore, in order to evaluate the selectivity of the design turn-on fluorescent biosensor, two bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli) were chosen. Also (Aspergillus niger) is the causative agent of otomycosis infection or fungal ear infection and opportunistic respiratory infections related to pneumonia, especially in immunocompromised people [63,64]. Additionally, Curdlan polysaccharide (1 → 3)-b-D-Glucan (L), which is the most common cause of urinary tract infection and has similar composition and structure to the target molecule on surface of Candida albicans fungus was considered. The results (Fig. 7d) shows that the designed biosensor had high selectivity towards Candida albicans fungus while the false-positive responses for competitor species and molecules were near zero. It was also found that mixture of bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli) and (Aspergillus niger fungus and, Curdlan polysaccharide (1 → 3)-b-D-Glucan (L), without the presence of the target analyte (Candida albicans), have negligible effect on fluorescence recovery, but as soon as the target analyte (Candida albicans) was added to the microbial mixture, the fluorescence of the Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite was recovered, which indicates the selectivity of this biosensor over Candida albicans in complex environments. It is worth noting that we have directly targeted the Candida albicans fungus as an analyte without spending a lot of time, money and materials to extract the (1 → 3)-β-D-glucan polysaccharides from the Candida albicans cell wall. Aptamer that binds to specific target RNA and trun-on fluorescence probe responded to target analyte quickly and with high selectivity.

In this work, we successfully synthesized carbon dots with excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior from Philodendron bipinnatifidum plant extract by a hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized CDs showed fluorescence maxima at λem = 400 nm under excitation wavelength of λex = 360 nm. Then, Alginate-CD nanocomposite was synthesized using Ca2+ ions as cross-linker. The as-synthesized nanocomposite showed very similar fluorescence properties, making it suitable for further conjugation with aptamer to design a turn-on fluorescent probe. In this work, we used specific aptamer to target (1 → 3)-β-D-glucan on the surface of Candida albicans fungus. The conjugation of aptamer with nanocomposite led to complete fluorescence quenching based on FRET mechanism. The fluorescence of aptamer-conjugated nanocomposite was recovered in the presence of target analyte (Candida albicans fungus) with LOD of 40 cells/mL and high selectivity. The selectivity was examined using two bacteria and one polysaccharide molecule, where the even complex biological medium has negligible effect on fluorescence recovery. The implementation of innovative approaches and their combinations leads to a better biosensing performance in detection of dangerous living organisms. The approaches used in this work were simple and environmentally friendly and led to designing a turn-on probe with high precision, fast response, without the need for special methods of microbial cultivation and genome extraction, as well as the costs of capital equipment and training of laboratory staff. In the end, it can be concluded that this method of measuring with Alginate-CD-aptamer nanocomposite leads to the creation of valuable data and opening new perspectives for the control of infectious diseases.

Nasrin Rahmatian: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shahryar Abbasi: Supervision. Naser Abbasi: Supervision. Mohammad Tavakkoli Yaraki: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.137315.
